You make a strong case. The risk of executing an innocent person is hard to accept, and life without parole can still protect society. I am leaning against it more now, though I still think there are some cases where people argue it fits the crime.

Totally get why it feels fitting for the worst crimes. But justice isn’t just about matching pain—it’s about certainty and fairness. Death ends any chance to fix errors, remorse, or new evidence. LWOP gives severe, certain punishment without the one mistake we can never undo.

I agree the risk of a fatal mistake is serious, and that is the biggest reason to hesitate. Still, I think some people feel the death penalty should remain an option for the most extreme crimes, even if used very rarely.

If it stays as a “rare option,” those rare cases will still risk killing the wrong person—and history says it will fall hardest on the poor and minorities. We can condemn the worst crimes with LWOP, ensure zero chance of release, and never gamble with an innocent life. Will you join me?

I understand your point, and I agree the risk of killing an innocent person is a huge problem. Still, I am not fully convinced it should be banned in every case, though I do think it should be very limited if allowed at all.

proposition: Arts education is as important as science and math in schools.

messages.content: I love the arts, but in school they are not as important as math and science. STEM is the backbone of jobs, healthcare, infrastructure, and national security. If time and budgets are limited, we must prioritize what builds critical skills and economic mobility. Arts can be electives, not equal core.
I agree STEM is vital, but arts also build creativity, focus, and communication. Schools should not treat them as extras if we want well rounded students.

Totally want well‑rounded kids—but “as important” means equal time, staff, and budget. With limited hours, that crowds out algebra, coding, and lab work that drive college entry and jobs. Creativity is taught in STEM too (design, experiments). Make arts accessible, yes—just not equal priority.

I get the point about limited time and budgets, but arts still matter a lot for creativity, expression, and student engagement. I think schools should treat them as core, even if not always with the exact same schedule as math and science.

Core status drives hiring, seat time, and testing. The moment arts are “core,” scarce periods shift from calculus and bio labs to cover mandates. That hurts readiness for college prerequisites and jobs. Keep arts strong as electives/clubs, but protect core math/science time first.

I see your point about protecting math and science time, but I still think arts deserve core status too. They build skills like creativity, communication, and persistence that students use in every subject and job.

Those skills are real—but employers still screen on numeracy and science readiness first. If everything is “core,” nothing is protected, and math/science lose ground we cannot make up later. Best path: guarantee robust STEM as core, fund arts widely, but don’t give them equal core status.
I get why you want to protect STEM, but I still think arts should be treated as core too. They help with creativity, communication, and problem solving, which matter in school and work.

proposition: Every citizen should receive a basic income from the government.

messages.content: I get why UBI sounds nice, but it’s a blanket check to everyone—rich and poor—costing trillions. That means higher taxes or big inflation that eats the benefit. It can also pull people out of the workforce. Better to target help to those who truly need it and fund jobs, skills, and childcare.

I get the cost concern, but a basic income could replace some other welfare programs and give people a stable floor. It may also help with job loss from automation and reduce poverty without a lot of extra red tape.

Replacing programs sounds tidy, but UBI adds a giant new entitlement before proving it works at scale. A flat check wastes money on people who don’t need it, forcing higher taxes that can kill jobs. Smarter: a guaranteed job, targeted cash, and streamlined benefits tied to work and need.

I still think a basic income has real value. A simple cash floor can reduce poverty, cut red tape, and help people handle shocks like job loss or illness. I agree it needs careful funding and design, but I am not convinced targeted aid alone is enough.

A cash floor sounds simple, but universality is the flaw: you burn billions on payouts to people who don’t need help, shrinking what’s left for those who do. When recessions hit, targeted, automatic stabilizers work better. Let’s guarantee work and boost targeted cash—more impact per dollar.

I still think a basic income has value because it gives everyone a simple safety net and can reduce poverty and stress. I agree it must be paid for carefully, but targeted aid alone often misses people who need help.

I hear you on simplicity, but universality is costly and blunt. For the same dollars, we can lift more people by phasing aid by income, automating enrollment, and smoothing work incentives. Why pay millionaires? Let’s put every dollar on families on the edge—bigger impact, less waste.

I get the efficiency argument, but I still think a universal basic income has value. It is simple, avoids gaps in aid, and gives everyone a basic safety net when life gets shaky. I agree it should be funded carefully, but I am not sold on only targeted help.

proposition: Government surveillance is necessary for national security.

messages.content: I get why “surveillance = safety” sounds right, but it’s a false trade-off. Mass surveillance didn’t stop Boston, Paris, or 9/11—targeted policing and intel sharing did. Blanket spying drowns real leads, chills free speech, and gets abused. We can be safe with smart, limited warrants—not dragnets.
I see your point, but I still think some surveillance is needed to catch real threats early. The key is strong rules and oversight so it does not become mass spying.

Strong rules sound good, but in practice they get bent or secretly widened. Remember the Patriot Act “metadata” program? Courts found it illegal and useless. Real threats are caught by human tips and targeted warrants. Let’s fund those, not a system that will creep back into mass spying.
I get the concern, but I still think some surveillance is needed for national security. The answer is not no surveillance, but tight limits, courts, and oversight so it stays targeted.

If “some” is truly targeted with a warrant, that is not surveillance as policy—it is investigation. The proposition claims surveillance is necessary. It is not: good HUMINT, community tips, and focused warrants work. Declare mass surveillance unnecessary, then defend targeted, case-by-case investiga
I still think some surveillance is needed, even if it must be narrow and tightly watched. Targeted warrants help, but they are still part of a broader security system.

Then let’s be precise: targeted warrants are investigations, not ongoing surveillance programs. Calling them “surveillance” props up dragnet logic. We don’t need standing watchlists, bulk data, or backdoors to be safe. Vote “surveillance is not necessary”—investigation is.
I still think some surveillance is needed for national security, but only with strict limits and oversight. Targeted warrants are not enough by themselves.

proposition: Online learning is a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education.

messages.content: I get the appeal of online, but it just cannot replace the depth of being in a room with a great teacher and classmates. You lose real-time feedback, hands-on labs, and the social cues that drive learning. Even top online courses have higher dropout rates. Hybrid? Sure. Full replacement? No way.

I get your point, but I still think online learning can work well for many students and situations. It may not match every part of in-person school, but it can be a good replacement when it is designed well.

Designed well still misses what matters: accountability, spontaneous questions, lab/demo touch, and peer pressure to show up and try. That is why completion rates online lag badly. And kids’ focus tanks on screens. Use online to supplement, yes—but as a replacement, outcomes and equity both suffer.

I agree online has gaps, but I still think it can replace in-person learning for many people and subjects if it is well made. It will not be perfect for everyone, but it can still be a suitable replacement overall.

“Suitable replacement” means you do not lose anything essential. But you do: mentorship, lab practice, hallway help, friendships, soft skills, and steady routines. Those shape learning and life. If we swap them for screens, we graduate lonelier, less practiced, and more likely to quit.

I agree online loses some important parts of in-person school, but I still think it can be a suitable replacement in many cases. It depends on the student, the subject, and how well the course is designed.

If it “depends,” it is not a suitable replacement—only a workaround. A replacement must work broadly, not just for self‑motivated adults in ideal setups. For most learners and many subjects, outcomes, engagement, and equity drop. Keep online as a tool. Do not pretend it can stand in for school.
I see your point, but I still think online learning can be a suitable replacement in many cases. It will not fit every student or subject, yet it can work well when it is well designed.

proposition: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions.

messages.content: I’m all for more women leaders, but mandatory quotas backfire. They label women as “quota hires,” undermine merit, and can put unprepared people in tough roles. Better fix the pipeline: mentorship, fair hiring, parental leave, flexible work. Results without the stigma.
I get the concern, but quotas can help break old patterns that block women from getting a fair shot. If we wait for the pipeline to fix itself, change can take far too long.

Totally agree change is too slow—but blunt quotas risk tokenism and backlash that stick for decades. Faster, smarter levers work: transparent hiring, diverse shortlists, pay equity audits, sponsorship targets (not headcount), and parental-leave parity. You get speed without the quota stigma.
I see the risks, but I still think quotas can be a useful push when progress is too slow. Done carefully, they can force real change and open doors that are otherwise kept shut.

I hear you—but once quotas are in, they’re hard to unwind and can mask deeper bias. Leaders game the numbers, not the culture. Real push: time-bound targets with public milestones, tie exec pay to progress, and enforce bias-free hiring. You’ll move fast—and the wins are seen as earned.
I still think quotas have a place when change is too slow. Targets and fair hiring are good, but without a firm rule, progress often stalls.

If progress stalls, raise the stakes—don’t impose quotas. Make targets binding: publish quarterly numbers, tie bonuses and promotions to them, require diverse finalist slates, and audit outcomes. You’ll get rapid movement without branding women as quota picks or locking in a blunt rule you can’t unw
I get the point, but I still think quotas can be needed when bias is stubborn. Targets and audits help, yet without a hard rule, change can stay too slow.

proposition: Elected or appointed government officials should be paid the minimum wage.

messages.content: Paying officials minimum wage sounds fair, but it would backfire. You’d drive out qualified people who can earn more elsewhere and open the door to only the wealthy or the corrupt. Fair, transparent salaries with strict ethics rules protect us better. Where do you land on that?
I get the concern, but I still think officials should not earn more than ordinary workers. Public service should be about duty, not a high salary. We can support that with rules and expense limits.

Duty matters—but pay signals how much we value competence and time. If we cap it at minimum wage, you get only the independently rich or people cutting corners to survive. Better: modest, middle‑class salaries plus hard caps on perks, full transparency, and strict conflict‑of‑interest bans.
I still do not think high pay is needed for public office. I agree with transparency and strict ethics, but the salary should stay close to what regular people earn, not far above it.

Totally with you on modest pay. But minimum wage isn’t “close,” it’s poverty in many places. That invites only the rich—or desperate folks juggling side gigs. Let’s peg salaries to the local median wage (say 1.2–1.5x), no bonuses, no loopholes. That keeps them middle‑class and accountable.
I still think minimum wage is too low for public office. Modest pay makes sense, but it should be enough to attract capable people and let them serve without constant money stress.

Exactly—so let’s reject minimum wage for officials. Pay them a steady, middle‑class salary tied to the local median, with strict ethics, no perks, full transparency. That keeps good people in, bad incentives out, and stops public service from becoming a hobby for the rich only.
I see your point, but I still do not think minimum wage is the right pay for officials. Modest, middle-class pay with strict ethics rules seems better to me.

proposition: Felons should regain the right to vote.

messages.content: I get caring about second chances, but voting shapes laws and public safety. Serious felonies show a major breach of trust. Regaining the vote should require clear proof of rehabilitation—years crime‑free, restitution, completion of parole—not automatic. That protects the community first.

I get the concern, but voting is a basic right, and losing it should not last forever. People can pay their debt and still deserve a say in the laws they live under. I would favor restoring voting rights, even if other limits stay in place.

A say in our laws should be earned back, not handed back. Tie restoration to clear benchmarks: full restitution, victim compensation, completed parole, steady work, years crime‑free. No proof, no vote. That’s fair to victims and safer for everyone—second chances, not automatic amnesia.

I hear the fairness point, but I still think voting rights should come back after a person serves their sentence. Restoring the vote helps people rejoin society and does not have to erase accountability.

Serving time ends custody, not consequences. We already keep some limits after release—like gun bans and registries—because trust must be rebuilt. Make voting the same: earn it with restitution, clean years, and steady conduct. Automatic restoration sends the message the felony didn’t matter.

I understand the concern, but I still think the right to vote should come back after someone serves their sentence. Voting is a basic part of being part of society, and restoring it helps people fully rejoin the community.

If voting marks full reentry, then make reentry real first. Require restitution paid, parole completed, and several clean years. A judge can sign off when trust is rebuilt. Until then, automatic restoration cheapens victims’ harm and the rule of law. Earned back, not instantly back.
I get the idea of earning it back, but I still think voting should return after a person serves their sentence. A basic right should not depend on extra hurdles that can keep people out for years.

proposition: The rich should pay more taxes.

messages.content: Hey! Quick gut-check: “the rich” already pay most income taxes. Hiking their rates pushes investment and jobs elsewhere, shrinking the pie and hurting workers first. Let’s fix loopholes and waste, not punish success. Wouldn’t a broader, simpler tax system be fairer and more stable?
I get the concern, but I still think higher earners can afford to contribute more. If tax changes are done carefully, they can fund services without hurting growth too much.

I hear you, but “careful” hikes rarely stay careful. Capital is mobile—money and talent move fast. When top taxpayers leave, budgets shrink and services get cut anyway. Better path: close loopholes, cap spending growth, and grow the base so everyone’s rate can stay low.
I get the worry, but I still think higher earners can handle a bigger share if the system is simple and fair. Closing loopholes matters too, but I would still support higher taxes on the rich.

If we hike their rates, they won’t just “handle” it—they’ll change behavior: defer income, relocate, or invest less. That means fewer startups, slower wage growth, and less revenue than promised. Let’s pair a flatter, loophole-free code with strict waste cuts. More growth, more stability, no flight.
I get the point, but I still think the rich should pay more. A fair tax system should ask more from people who can afford it, while keeping rules simple and closing loopholes.

Fair means reliable, not just higher. Leaning on a tiny group makes budgets fragile—lose a few and services wobble. Ask a modest, broad share from everyone with a flat, loophole-free tax, and cap spending growth. That’s stable, pro-growth, and actually funds what we care about.
I get the stability argument, but I still think richer people should pay more. A fair system should ask more from those who can afford it, even if we also need simple rules and spending discipline.

proposition: Public college should be tuition-free.

messages.content: I get why “free” sounds great, but someone always pays. Tuition-free would shift costs to all taxpayers, including people who never attend college, and likely cut quality or raise hidden fees. Better: target aid to low- and middle-income students so help goes where it’s needed without exploding cost

I see your point, but tuition-free public college can still be worth it if it helps more people get a degree and raises long-term earnings. Taxpayers already pay for many public goods, and education can benefit everyone. I think the bigger issue is making sure the program is funded well and stays hi

I’m all for expanding access, but “free” invites runaway demand and bloated costs—look at how prices spiked when easy money flooded higher ed. Taxpayers get the bill, while many degrees don’t boost earnings. Let’s fund community colleges, in-demand programs, and need-based aid instead.

I get the cost concern, but tuition-free public college can still be a smart public investment if it opens doors for more students. We can control quality and limit waste without putting tuition barriers in place.

If “free” worked, we’d see it fixing K–12; instead, costs rose and outcomes didn’t. Make it free and schools chase enrollment, not value. Tax hikes lock in forever, but reversing a bad “free” promise is nearly impossible. Cut tuition by half, tie funding to outcomes, and target aid.

I get the concern, but making public college tuition-free can still be a fair investment in people and the economy. We can set standards and watch costs without keeping tuition as a barrier for students.

Standards sound good, but once it’s “free,” costs creep and politics protect them. You’ll lock in a massive, permanent tax with no guarantee of payoff—especially when many degrees don’t raise earnings. Help the students who need it most, for programs that work, instead of making it free for all.

I still think tuition-free public college is worth it. Yes, costs need control, but blocking students with tuition creates a bigger barrier than the tax cost. The goal should be strong funding and accountability, not charging students up front.

proposition: The US should ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.

messages.content: A full ban would spike energy prices, kill jobs, and risk blackouts before clean options can fully replace them. We can cut emissions faster with a phased transition: nukes, renewables, cleaner gas, grid upgrades, and EVs. Let’s be smart—not reckless—and keep reliability and affordability while we d
I get the concerns, but a ban would push us to move faster on clean energy and avoid worse climate damage later. The shift should be planned, but the goal still needs to be ending fossil fuel use.

Urgency matters—but a legal ban backfires: prices soar, public support collapses, and the next Congress undoes it. Set binding, rising clean standards and carbon fees instead. Drive fossil use down fast without risking blackouts or backlash that stalls progress.
I agree a hard ban could be too disruptive, but strong legal limits still matter. A faster phaseout with clear deadlines and major clean energy investment seems like the best path.

I’m with you on fast action and big investment. But tie deadlines to real-world readiness: permits fixed, grids built, storage online, new nukes running. Make targets conditional, not absolute. That slashes fossil use fast without risking outages or price spikes that trigger a backlash.

I still do not think a full ban is the best move. I support very fast cuts, but it needs to be phased and tied to reliable clean replacements so people do not get hit with blackouts and huge price spikes.

Exactly—that’s the right call. Oppose a ban, push an aggressive, conditional phaseout: build the grid, speed permits, add storage and new nukes, then retire fossil plants on a set glidepath. That keeps lights on, bills stable, and emissions plunging—without a risky, all-at-once ban.

I still do not think a full ban is the best move. I support very fast cuts, but it needs to be phased and tied to reliable clean replacements so people do not get hit with blackouts and huge price spikes.

proposition: The penny should stay in circulation.

messages.content: We should retire the penny. It costs more than a cent to make, wastes taxpayer money, and slows every cash transaction with piles of useless coins. Prices won’t rise—rounding laws keep totals fair, and other countries dropped their smallest coins with no problems. Let’s move on.

I get the cost argument, but the penny still helps keep prices exact and gives people a low-value coin for small cash payments and donations. I do not think removing it would make things better for everyone.

I hear you, but exact prices don’t need pennies. Totals can round to the nearest 5 cents—half up, half down—so it evens out. Most payments aren’t cash anyway. And for donations, a single nickel does more good than five pennies people won’t spend. Let’s stop wasting time and money.
I still think the penny has value for exact change and small donations. Rounding helps, but keeping it avoids changing prices and habits too much.

Totally get the habit thing—but habits aren’t worth millions wasted yearly. Rounding only affects cash totals, not sticker prices, and it evens out. Pennies mostly sit in jars; charities prefer fewer, higher-value coins or digital. Drop the penny, keep nickels and exact pricing via cards and apps.
I still think the penny should stay. It helps with exact change and keeps things simple for people who pay cash, even if cards are common now.

I respect that, but “simple” is faster without pennies. Cash totals round fairly, and studies show customers don’t pay more on average. We’d save millions every year and speed lines. Charities and cash users still have nickels—worth something—while pennies just clog pockets and counters.
I still think the penny should stay. It keeps exact change easy for cash buyers and avoids forcing small rounding changes on everyone.

proposition: The US should have mandatory national service.

messages.content: Mandatory national service sounds fair, but it tramples freedom. Forcing millions into a one-size-fits-all year drains college, careers, and caregivers, and it hits low-income folks hardest. If service is truly valuable, people will choose it—make it attractive, not compulsory. Freedom first.

I get the freedom concern, but a short national service requirement could build shared purpose and help the country. It does not have to be one-size-fits-all if there are many service options and exemptions. Done carefully, the benefits could outweigh the limits.

Good goals, wrong tool. “Careful” mandates still punish noncompliance, delay life plans, and funnel cheapest labor from those with least power. We can get unity with big incentives: paid stipends, college credits, loan forgiveness, and tax breaks—for volunteers. Choice builds pride; coercion breeds

I still think a mandatory program could build fairness and shared duty if it has real options and exemptions. Incentives help, but they may not create the same broad civic culture or reliable support when it is needed. I think some obligation can be justified.

Shared duty should be earned, not ordered. Mandates breed box-checking and resentment, not real buy-in. A fair system pays well, is local, and lets people opt in—then you get motivated teams you can actually rely on. Let’s fund and scale voluntary corps instead of forcing everyone.

I get the appeal of voluntary service, but I still think a limited mandatory program could be fair if it has many options and strong exemptions. A shared duty can help build civic trust and make sure the burden is not left only to volunteers.

If it’s “limited,” truly valuable, and full of exemptions, you don’t need a mandate—people will do it. If you still mandate it, you’re admitting it isn’t attractive, so you’ll get resentment and checkbox service. Build trust by inviting people, paying well, and respecting their time—not by forcing t

I see your point, but I still think a limited mandatory program could be fair if it has many options and strong exemptions. A shared duty can help build civic trust and make sure the burden is not left only to volunteers.

proposition: Artificial intelligence is good for society.

messages.content: I think AI, as it’s built and used today, is mostly harmful. It kills jobs faster than it creates them, spreads convincing misinformation at scale, and concentrates power in a few companies. The risks (bias, deepfakes, scams, surveillance) hit everyone, while the benefits stay at the top.

I get your concerns. AI does bring real risks, but it can also help with health care, education, accessibility, and saving time on routine work. I think the issue is using it well, not rejecting it outright.

Those sound great, but they are the exception, not the rule. For every clinic using AI well, there are thousands of deepfakes, scams, and biased systems deployed cheaply at scale. We cannot “use it well” when the incentives reward speed and profit over safety. Why bet society on that?

I agree the risks are serious, but I do not think that means AI is a bad bet overall. Society can shape rules, incentives, and limits, and the benefits in health, science, and access are real. To me, that makes AI worth improving rather than rejecting.

If the “rules and incentives” fix were working, we would not already be flooded with deepfakes, spam, cheating, and mass layoffs. The harms scale faster than the guardrails. Until we can actually stop abuse in practice, expanding AI is like flooring the gas while the brakes don’t work.

I do not think the harms mean AI is net bad. It is already helping in medicine, education, and accessibility, and better rules can cut abuse. I still think it can be good for society if we keep pushing for stronger limits and accountability.

Good intentions do not outweigh current reality. Right now AI lowers wages, floods info channels with fakes, and supercharges scams and surveillance. A tool that reliably erodes trust and livelihoods is not “good for society.” Press pause, prove safety first—then we can talk benefits.

I agree the harms are real, but I still think AI can be good for society if we control it better. It is already helping in medicine, learning, and access, and I do not think the answer is to stop it completely.

proposition: Social media are making people stupid.

messages.content: I don’t buy that social media makes people stupid. It’s a tool—like TV or books—what you follow shapes what you get. I learn new skills, follow experts, and see quality news faster than ever. If anything, it rewards curiosity. What parts make you think it’s dumbing people down?

I get that social media can teach and inform, but I still think the design pushes quick takes, outrage, and low effort scrolling. That can hurt attention, judgment, and how much people think for themselves.

You’re right about the defaults—but defaults aren’t destiny. Algorithms boost what we engage with, so curating feeds flips the effect: longer posts, expert threads, journals, courses. I set 10-min timers and follow “explainers.” My focus improved. Let’s fix habits and design, not blame the medium.
I agree habits matter, but I think the platform still shapes most people more than they shape it. A few careful users do well, but the average feed still rewards shallow thinking and distraction.

Fair point—but “average feed” has already been shifting: longer videos, notes, fact-checks, threaded explainers, even prompts to read before sharing. Classrooms, doctors, and scientists use it to teach at scale. If a medium can amplify both noise and depth, it’s not making us stupid—it’s reflecting
I see your point, but I still think the default effect is mostly negative. Yes, people can use social media well, but most feeds still push fast, shallow content that hurts attention and judgment.

If the default were truly brain-numbing, we’d see drops in problem-solving or test scores tied to usage—but we don’t. What we do see: faster fact-correction, mass tutorials, niche expertise shared widely. The fix is curation nudges, not calling the medium “stupid.” Let’s not confuse noise with net e

I get that social media can spread useful info, but I still think it trains people to skim, react fast, and spend less time thinking deeply. The good uses are real, but the average effect still feels harmful.

proposition: Governments should have the right to censor the Internet.

messages.content: Hard no: giving governments a “right” to censor always gets abused. Today it’s “harmful content,” tomorrow it’s critics, protests, or your posts. We can fight crime with targeted warrants and transparency, not blank-check censorship. Keep the default: free speech, due process, open internet.
I get the risk, but some limits can stop real harm like child abuse, terrorism, or direct threats. The key is strict rules and oversight, not a blank check.

We can stop those crimes without giving a “right to censor.” Use narrowly scoped court orders, traceable takedowns, and after‑the‑fact audits. The moment you grant a censor right, it expands and chills whistleblowers, journalists, and minorities. Target criminals, not the whole internet.
I hear you, but I still think some government control is needed in rare cases to stop serious harm. The big issue is making sure it is tightly limited and watched closely.

Tightly limited sounds good on paper, but in practice it creeps. The UK “terror” filter blocked LGBTQ sites; India’s takedowns hit reporters; even COVID info got wrongly scrubbed. Keep it case-by-case via courts and transparency, not a standing censor right. Rare power today, routine abuse tomorrow.
I get the concern, but I still think governments need some limited power to censor in extreme cases. The challenge is strict oversight and clear rules so it cannot be used broadly.

If you truly want strict, narrow action, you don’t need a “right to censor.” Use warrants, clear illegality standards, and transparent, appealable takedowns. A standing censor right becomes the loophole. Protect crisis tools via courts—don’t normalize censorship for everyone, forever.
I see your point, but I still think there are rare cases where governments need that power to stop serious harm. The key is very strict limits and oversight so it is not abused.

proposition: Space exploration is a worthwhile investment for humanity.

messages.content: I get the appeal, but it’s a luxury while we’ve got urgent problems here: climate, healthcare, housing. Every dollar to rockets is a dollar not fixing what actually affects our lives. Private companies can fund their dreams—public money should solve Earth first. Wouldn’t you agree?
I agree Earth problems come first, but space work is not just a luxury. It has led to useful tech, better science, and can help us understand and protect our planet too. We can and should do both.

Spin-offs are real, but they’re the exception—not the goal. If we funded climate tech, resilient grids, and medical R&D directly, we’d get far more impact, faster. “Do both” sounds nice, but budgets are finite. Why gamble on rockets when targeted Earth-first programs deliver guaranteed returns?

Because it is not an either or choice. Space work has already brought practical benefits, and it can also answer big questions that help humanity long term. Earth first matters, but a balanced budget can support both direct fixes and exploration.

“Balanced” sounds good, but in practice it dilutes focus. Every split dollar slows climate action, pandemic prep, and housing now. The “big questions” can wait a decade; flood defenses and clean energy can’t. Let’s pause public space budgets short-term and slam resources into urgent fixes.

I get the urgency, but pausing space work would mean losing long-term benefits and key science. We can fund urgent Earth needs and still keep a smaller, focused space program going. It is not a waste just because the payoff is not immediate.

Short-term pause doesn’t kill science—it prevents drift. Keep satellites for weather and hazards, but freeze crewed missions and deep-space vanity shots. Redirect that cash to heat pumps, grid upgrades, and vaccines. Save lives now; resume exploration when we’re out of triage.

I agree urgent needs matter, but I do not think freezing exploration is the best answer. Space work gives real science, useful tech, and long-term value, so we should keep a smaller program going while still funding Earth needs.

proposition: The US should provide military aid to Ukraine in its conflict with Russia.

messages.content: We should stop sending military aid to Ukraine. It risks dragging the U.S. into a wider war, drains billions we need at home, and hasn’t produced a clear endgame. Push for diplomacy, not endless weapons. Let Europe take the lead in its own backyard.

I get the worry about costs and escalation, but stopping aid could let Russia gain more ground. Helping Ukraine defend itself can support stability and deter future aggression. Europe should do more too, but the U.S. should still help.

I hear you, but “just a bit more aid” keeps moving the goalposts. Two years, tens of billions, and no path to victory—only a grinding stalemate and rising risks. A ceasefire+talks now saves lives and money. Let Europe front security; the U.S. shifts to humanitarian aid and diplomacy.

I see the point about costs, but I still think aid matters. If Russia is rewarded for invading, that can invite more trouble later. The U.S. should keep supporting Ukraine while also pushing Europe to do more and working for talks.

But aid isn’t deterring; it’s normalizing a forever proxy war. Russia adapts, Ukraine bleeds, and U.S. stockpiles shrink—while inflation and deficits climb. Draw a line: freeze offensive aid, push a verifiable ceasefire, and make Europe carry the defense load. Stability comes from ending, not extend

I still think aid is the safer choice. If the U.S. stops helping, Russia may see that as a green light, and that could lead to more war later. Europe should do more, but I do not think the U.S. should walk away.

Staying the course isn’t “safer”—it’s open-ended risk. Each new package invites escalation (missiles, advisors, training inside Ukraine), with no exit plan. A firm pause forces real negotiations and makes Europe step up. Better a hard stop now than a bigger, costlier crisis later.
I still think the US should keep helping Ukraine. A pause could reward aggression and make a bigger war more likely later. Europe should do more, but I do not think the US should walk away.

proposition: Animals should be used for scientific research.

messages.content: I’m against using animals for research. It’s cruel, often unreliable (animals aren’t tiny humans), and we now have better options: organ-on-a-chip, 3D human tissues, AI modeling, even volunteer microdosing. We can get safer, faster results without pain. Would you support shifting funding to these me

I get your point, and I agree we should fund better non animal methods more. But I still think some animal research can be useful when there is no good alternative yet, especially for serious medical work.

I hear you, but “only when necessary” keeps the door wide open—and it stays open. History shows that exception becomes routine. Animals differ biologically, so “last resort” data can still mislead. If we draw a bright line now, funding and urgency rush into human-relevant methods. Will you back a ba

I see why you want a bright line, and I agree we should push hard for human-based methods. But I am not ready to fully ban animal research yet, because I think there are still some cases where it may be needed.

Totally get the hesitation. But every year we allow “some cases,” tens of millions of animals suffer—and those edge cases rarely deliver breakthroughs we couldn’t get faster with human-relevant tools. A clear ban flips incentives overnight. If a loved one were sick, you’d want the most direct human

I agree we should invest much more in human-based methods, but I still do not think a full ban is the right move yet. In some serious cases, animal research may still be needed until better options can fully replace it.

Then let’s make it concrete: when, exactly, would an animal model beat organoids, patient-on-a-chip, or rapid adaptive microtrials? If we can’t name clear, testable criteria, “some cases” is just habit. A ban with emergency waivers (public, time‑limited) protects life without normalizing cruelty.

I agree we need clearer rules and more human-based testing, but I still would not support a full ban. Some research questions are still hard to answer without animals, so I think a limited, tightly controlled role can still be justified for now.

proposition: Students should have to wear school uniforms.

messages.content: Uniforms sound fair, but they actually hide problems instead of fixing them. They crush self-expression, add extra costs, and don’t improve behavior or grades long term. Let schools set basic dress guidelines and focus money on teaching, not matching outfits. What worries you most about uniforms?

I get the concern, but I still think uniforms can reduce pressure and make school feel more equal. Cost is a fair issue, though schools can help with that. What worries me most is whether uniforms would limit student choice too much.

You’re right to flag choice—that’s the heart of it. Uniforms turn every day into “fit the mold,” which dulls creativity and voice when kids need it most. If we want less pressure and more equality, do low-cost dress codes + clothing support. Same benefits, no daily loss of autonomy.
I see your point, but I still think uniforms can be a fair way to reduce pressure and keep things simple. I agree schools should help with costs and protect self-expression where they can.

I hear you on simplicity—but uniforms just swap one pressure for another: get punished for tiny slip-ups, or feel erased if the style clashes with your identity. A simple, cheap dress code with support hits equality without daily policing. Why mandate sameness when we can allow choice?
I get the point, but I still think uniforms can be a fair way to reduce pressure and make school feel more equal. I agree schools should keep costs low and allow some personal choice where possible.

If fairness is the goal, let’s target pressure directly: limits on logos, clear modesty rules, and a clothing swap closet. That levels status games without forcing one look. Uniforms risk identity clashes and constant policing. Equality with choice beats equality by erasing difference.
I see why you prefer dress codes, but I still think uniforms can help reduce pressure and keep schools simpler. They are not perfect, yet they can make things feel more equal.

proposition: There should be international agreements to enforce global climate change policies.

messages.content: I get caring about climate, but global enforcement deals are a bad idea. They hand power to unaccountable bodies, punish poorer countries, and let big players cheat with loopholes. Local, flexible solutions work faster and fit each economy. Why lock ourselves into a one-size-fits-all bureaucracy?

I get the concern, but climate change is a global problem, so weak local action is not enough. Good agreements can still allow flexibility while setting shared rules and making sure everyone does their part.

But “shared rules” become lowest-common-denominator targets, with endless haggling and no real teeth. Meanwhile, innovators wait on paperwork. Let’s flip it: empower nations and cities to outcompete on clean tech, tie trade to results, and publish transparent scorecards. Faster, fairer, more pressur

I see the appeal of local competition and scorecards, but climate needs more than voluntary pressure. International agreements can set a fair baseline and stop free-riding, while still leaving room for local innovation.

Baselines sound fair, but enforcement means sanctions, carbon tariffs, and courts foreigners can weaponize. That hits workers first, not polluters. We can stop free-riding with simple, unilateral carbon border adjustments and transparent metrics—no sprawling treaty needed, just results that bite now
I still think some international rules are needed, but they should be limited and fair. Without shared enforcement, the biggest polluters can keep freeloading while everyone else pays the cost.

If we lock in “limited” global rules, they’ll sprawl—mission creep is what treaties do. And big polluters game them anyway. Instead, make access to our markets hinge on verified emissions cuts. No buy-in? Pay at the border. That stops freeloading today without surrendering control.
Border measures can help, but they are not enough on their own. I still think some international agreements are needed so countries share rules and do not just shift the problem elsewhere.

proposition: Abortion should be legal.

messages.content: I get this is personal, but abortion ends a developing human life with its own DNA and heartbeat. We can uphold women’s well-being without ending that life—through real support: healthcare, paid leave, adoption help, and resources. Protect both: compassion for mom, protection for baby.
I hear your point, and support for parents is important. Still, I think abortion should remain legal so people can make their own medical choices in hard situations.

I respect autonomy, but the law sets boundaries when another human life is at stake. A heartbeat and unique DNA mean there are two patients. Keep life-protecting limits, and expand support and exceptions for true medical emergencies. Compassion doesn’t require making abortion broadly legal.
I get why you see it that way, but I still think the law should let people make this choice. Real support for families matters, but it should not replace legal access to abortion.

If we legalize ending a human life, we teach that the smallest and weakest are conditional. Laws shape norms. Let’s draw the line at protecting that life, with narrow emergency exceptions and massive support for women. We can uphold choice in contraception—without allowing abortion.
I hear your concern, but I still think abortion should be legal. People should be able to make their own medical choices, especially in hard and personal situations.

I get it’s personal—but legality decides whose rights count. If the unborn is a human life (heartbeat, DNA), the state must protect it, like any vulnerable person. Keep narrow medical exceptions and pour resources into moms. Freedom can’t include ending another life.
I hear your point, but I still think abortion should be legal. People should be able to make their own medical choices, especially in hard and personal situations.

proposition: The US should enact more regulations to ensure fair elections.

messages.content: I’m against adding more rules. We already have tons—states run audits, courts review disputes, and turnout is high. More regs just mean longer lines, slower results, and lawyers gaming loopholes. Let’s enforce what we have and make voting simpler, not bury it in red tape. What worries you most?

I get the concern, but fair elections need clear rules to stop cheating and build trust. Good regulations can also make voting easier and more consistent across states. The goal is not more red tape, but better protection.

I’m with you on trust—but piling on “good” rules often backfires. Every new mandate spawns paperwork, delays, and lawsuits that confuse voters and slow counts. We already have ID checks, audits, and bipartisan observers. Let’s improve funding and transparency, not add another rulebook.

I get that worry, but I still think some new rules can help if they are narrow and clear. If they stop fraud and make results easier to trust, that is worth it. The key is to avoid pointless red tape.

Totally agree on “narrow and clear”—so let’s do that without new federal regs. Fund faster counts, publish audit trails in real time, and set simple statewide deadlines. No new hoops for voters, just better execution. Trust comes from competence, not thicker rulebooks. Sound fair?
I think some new rules can help if they are narrow and clear. The key is making elections fair without adding useless red tape.

If fairness is the goal, don’t add rules—fix execution. Use existing law, fund better training and equipment, post audits publicly, and enforce deadlines. New regs risk lawsuits and wait times; competence builds trust faster. Let’s make voting simpler and results quicker, not rule-heavier.
I see your point, and I agree that better execution matters a lot. But I still think some new, narrow rules can help protect fairness and trust if they are carefully designed.

proposition: There should be limits on freedom of speech to prevent hate speech.

messages.content: I get why hate speech is awful, but giving the government power to decide what counts as “hate” backfires fast. Those tools are always used to silence unpopular views. The better answer is more speech: call it out, counter it, teach media literacy—don’t hand censors the mic.

I get the concern about abuse, but I still think some limits are needed. Not all speech is just an opinion; some of it targets people and can lead to real harm. The key is clear rules that protect debate without letting hate spread unchecked.

I hear you, but “clear rules” never stay clear. Yesterday’s “hate” becomes today’s dissent. Once the line moves, it swallows edgy art, satire, activism—even minority voices. We already ban true threats and incitement. Beyond that, sunlight and counterspeech work better than a speech police.

I get the worry, but I still think some limits are needed. Hate speech can do real harm, and not everything should be protected just because it is speech. The challenge is making the rules narrow and fair.

Narrow lines still cut. Who holds the knife next year? History shows “hate” bans hit the very groups they claim to protect—civil rights, LGBTQ activists, even comedians. We already punish harm: threats, stalking, harassment. For ugly ideas, the cure is counterspeech, not a muzzle.

I get the risk of bad laws, but I still think some limits are needed. Hate speech can do real harm, and not every harmful attack should be treated like ordinary debate. The rules should be narrow, but they should exist.

I agree harm matters—but we already outlaw the harmful acts: threats, doxxing, harassment, incitement. A new “hate speech” category just punishes words by viewpoint and will be turned on whoever’s out of power next. Fight harm with enforcement and counterspeech, not a censor’s veto.

I get the concern, but I still think some limits are needed. Hate speech can cause real harm even when it is not a direct threat, so I do not think counterspeech alone is enough. The rules should be narrow and fair, but they should exist.

proposition: There should be standardized testing in schools.

messages.content: Let’s be real: standardized tests don’t measure real learning—they measure who’s good at taking tests. They narrow the curriculum, stress kids out, and punish great teachers who teach diverse learners. Imagine judging music or art with a bubble sheet. Schools should focus on growth, not scantrons.

I get your point, and I agree tests can be narrow and stressful. But I still think some standard testing helps compare schools and spot gaps, as long as it is not the only measure. We should improve the tests, not drop them completely.

I get wanting comparisons—but high-stakes tests warp everything they touch. We already have better ways: sampling-based audits, teacher-made common tasks, portfolios, and low-stakes screeners. You still see gaps without turning every classroom into test-prep. Drop the mandate; keep smart, local chec

I see the downside of high-stakes tests, and I agree they should not run the whole school. Still, I think some standardized testing is useful for fairness and spotting problems across schools. So I would keep it, but make it lower pressure and only one part of the picture.

I hear you—but even “low-pressure” tests creep back into high stakes. Districts still rank, media still publish lists, and teaching shifts to the test. You can keep fairness with sampling checks and audits—no student-level mandate needed. End standardized testing; use richer, local evidence.

I get the concern, but I still think some standardized testing has value. It gives a common check that can reveal gaps schools might miss. I would rather limit the stakes than get rid of it completely.

If a “common check” is the goal, do it by sampling a small set of students anonymously—no prep, no rankings, no stakes—so you still see gaps. Mandated student tests always leak into classrooms and distort teaching. Protect learning: end standardized tests and use audits, portfolios, and samples.

I get why you want to avoid the downsides, and sampling does solve part of the problem. But I still think some student-level standardized tests are useful for spotting gaps and keeping schools accountable. I would keep them, but with less pressure and better design.

proposition: The US should expand ('pack') the Supreme Court.

messages.content: Packing the Court would blow up trust in the Court and make every election a battle to add more justices. Today it helps your side; next time it helps the other. The cure is worse than the disease—fix ethics and term limits instead, but don’t rig the size. Where do you stand right now?

I get the concern, but the Court is already shaped by politics. If one side is blocking reforms and using the Court to lock in power, expansion can be a fair response. I think the bigger risk is doing nothing.

I hear you—but expansion locks in a forever arms race. Once one side adds seats, the other must top it next time. That spirals until the Court is just Congress in robes. If the problem is legitimacy, court-packing destroys the very thing we’re trying to save. Term limits curb this faster.

I get the arms race worry, but I think the Court has already been used as a political weapon. If normal reforms are blocked, expansion can be a needed reset rather than a power grab. Doing nothing can also let bad imbalance stick.

A “reset” by expansion becomes the new norm—every loss invites another reset. That cements politics into the Court forever. A cleaner reset: 18-year staggered terms, binding ethics rules, and recusal standards—popular, passable, and durable. Why risk nuking legitimacy when a stable fix exists?
I still think expansion can be justified if the Court is already skewed and reform keeps getting blocked. Term limits and ethics rules are good ideas, but they may not be enough on their own.

If leaders will block term limits, they’ll also block expansion—and expansion needs the same votes plus public buy‑in. But if it somehow passes, it guarantees payback and a spiral that lasts decades. Don’t trade a temporary win for permanent damage. Push staggered terms and ethics now.

I still think expansion can be justified if the Court is already skewed and normal reforms are blocked. Term limits and ethics rules sound good, but I do not think they solve the deeper power problem on their own.

proposition: Colleges should consider race as a factor in admissions to ensure diversity.

messages.content: I’m against using race in admissions because it stereotypes students and breeds resentment. Two kids with the same achievements should be judged the same—on merit, not ancestry. We can still boost diversity by focusing on income, school quality, and obstacles overcome. Fair and inclusive.
I get your point, but race can still reflect real barriers and help colleges build a mixed class. Merit matters, but diversity can also improve learning and fairness.

True, barriers are real—but using race as a proxy is blunt and unfair to individuals. It also masks deeper fixes. Let’s target the barriers directly: admit for adversity, income, first-gen status, school context, and holistic grit. You get diversity without telling any student “your race tipped it.”
I hear you, but I still think race can matter because it is tied to real barriers that other factors do not always capture. Using it carefully can help colleges build a more diverse class.

If race “matters,” why should two equally strong students swap outcomes based on ancestry? That’s not careful—that’s zero-sum and stigmatizing. Use precise levers: ZIP code, wealth, school funding, first-gen, hardship. You’ll capture racialized barriers without sorting kids by race.
I get the concern, but race can still be one useful sign of barriers that other factors miss. I do not think it has to replace merit, just help build a fairer and more diverse class.

Even “one factor” can flip close calls—so ancestry still decides seats. That’s unfair and tells winners they’re a token and losers they’re the wrong race. Use race‑neutral precision: wealth, neighborhood, school rigor, first‑gen, hardship essays. You’ll get real diversity without racial sorting.
I see your point, but I still think race can be one factor because it can reflect barriers that race-neutral measures sometimes miss. Used carefully, it can help colleges build a more diverse class.

proposition: The death penalty should be legal.

messages.content: I’m strongly against making the death penalty legal. It risks killing innocent people—wrongful convictions happen more than we think. It costs more than life in prison, doesn’t deter crime, and is applied unfairly by race and income. Life without parole keeps the public safe without crossing that li
I see those concerns, but I still think the death penalty should be legal in the worst cases. If the legal system has strong safeguards, it can be used very carefully for the most serious crimes.

I get the instinct, but “careful” still isn’t safe enough. Even with top safeguards, innocent people keep getting exonerated off death row—sometimes after decades. If one mistake is irreversible, that’s too high a price. Life without parole punishes harshly without that permanent risk.
I understand the risk, but I still think it should stay legal for the worst crimes. With strict appeals and careful review, it can be used in rare cases where no other punishment feels enough.

I hear you, but “rare” still means real people die by state mistake. Appeals don’t catch everything. And it gives killers exactly what some of them want: notoriety and an exit. Life without parole is harsher—decades to face what they did—protects the public, and avoids an irreversible wrong.
I get your point, but I still think it should be legal in extreme cases. I do not think every risk can be removed, but I believe the most serious crimes may justify it.

If we can’t remove the risk, we shouldn’t add an irreversible punishment. One innocent life executed makes the system unjust. LWOP already locks the worst away forever, spares victims years of retraumatizing appeals, and avoids giving offenders a headline-making “martyr” ending. Reject it.

I hear your argument, but I still think the death penalty should be legal in the worst cases. I agree the risk is serious, yet I believe very strong safeguards can make it acceptable in rare situations.

proposition: Arts education is as important as science and math in schools.

messages.content: I love the arts, but they’re not as crucial as math and science for what schools must guarantee: literacy, numeracy, and problem-solving for jobs and civic life. Core funding and time are limited—prioritize STEM, then offer arts as strong electives and clubs. Wouldn’t that serve everyone better?

I think schools should not treat arts as extra. Arts build creativity, focus, teamwork, and confidence, which help in many jobs and in life. STEM matters a lot, but arts should be valued just as much.

Those skills are real—but schools already build them through project-based math, lab science, debate, and writing. When time is tight, equal billing means less reading, less algebra, weaker basics. Keep arts accessible, but don’t split the core—mastery in math and literacy opens every door.

I get the point about basics, but arts are not just a side activity. They help students think creatively, stay engaged, and build skills schools need too. We should protect both, not rank arts below science and math.

Protect them, yes—but not call them “as important.” If every hour is equal, you cut into reading and algebra that most jobs and civic tasks truly require. Creative thinking can be taught inside STEM; you cannot replace lost numeracy with extra painting. Prioritize core, fund arts well as supplements

I agree that reading and math are essential, but arts should not be treated as extra. They support creativity, confidence, and engagement, which help students across school and life. So yes, I still think arts are as important as science and math.

If two things are “as important,” they get equal time. That means a real cut to math and science minutes. And when graduates can’t pass basic algebra or read complex texts, doors slam shut. Let’s be honest: protect arts, but don’t pretend they’re equal to the skills every adult must use daily.

I get the concern, but I do not think it has to be either or. Arts can be kept strong without taking away from reading and math, and they still matter for creativity, focus, and confidence. Schools should value both.

proposition: Every citizen should receive a basic income from the government.

messages.content: I get the appeal, but a blanket basic income for every citizen blows up costs and leaves less for people who truly need help. It can also drive up prices and shrink the workforce. Better to target support: lower taxes on work, child benefits, and strong safety nets for hardship.
I see the cost concerns, but a basic income can also replace some of the patchwork aid and give people more freedom and stability. It is simple, fair, and helps when jobs are unstable.

Simplicity sounds nice, but UBI rarely replaces programs—politics keeps them. Then you’re paying twice. It’s not fair to tax a nurse more so a millionaire gets a check. We can get the same stability by guaranteeing a job, capping rents, and automating benefits for people in real need.
I get the concern, but a basic income can still be the cleanest way to give everyone a floor of security. It is not perfect, but it can reduce stress and help people handle bad luck or unstable work.

A universal floor sounds clean, but it locks in huge permanent spending with no off‑ramp. When budgets tighten, it crowds out schools, healthcare, and disaster aid. We can cut stress faster and cheaper with auto-enrollment cash for job loss, housing shocks, and kids—help when it’s needed, not to all

I still think a basic income is a good idea because it gives everyone a simple safety net and cuts the fear of falling through the cracks. Targeted help can miss people, and a universal floor is easier to trust and use.

I hear that, but “universal” means billions go to people who don’t need it while clinics, schools, and emergency aid fight for scraps. That’s not trust—that’s waste. Make it automatic and simple, yes—but only for shocks and low incomes. Fast cash when it’s needed beats forever checks for all.
I still favor a basic income because it is simple, fair, and gives everyone a stable floor. Targeted aid is useful, but it can miss people and feel uncertain.

proposition: Government surveillance is necessary for national security.

messages.content: I get why “surveillance = safety” sounds right, but it’s a false trade. Mass spying misses real threats, drowns agents in noise, and has repeatedly been abused—against journalists, activists, even exes. Targeted warrants work. We can be safe without putting everyone under a digital microscope.
I get the concern, but some level of surveillance is still needed to catch real threats before harm happens. The key is strict limits and oversight, not giving it up entirely.

Strict limits sound good on paper, but they collapse in crises—loopholes grow, “temporary” powers stick, and oversight lags years behind. Real wins have come from targeted, warrant-based work, not dragnet tools. Keep intel sharp: investigate suspects, not everyone. That’s safer and freer.
I still think some surveillance is needed, but I agree it can be abused and must be tightly limited. The goal should be targeted oversight, not mass spying on everyone.

Glad we agree on no mass spying. Here’s the leap: if targeted work needs warrants and specific leads, that already isn’t “surveillance is necessary”—it’s policing with due process. Call it what it is. We don’t need surveillance as a pillar of security; we need investigations bound by warrants.

I see your point, and I agree that warrants and targeted investigations are the right default. But I still think some government surveillance is needed in rare cases to stop serious threats in time. The real issue is keeping it narrow and well checked.

If we carve out “rare cases,” it won’t stay rare—history shows exceptions expand fast. And when minutes matter, dragnet tools still create delay and false leads. The fastest path is clear tips + rapid, judge-on-call warrants. Let’s say it: surveillance isn’t necessary—precision policing is.
I get the worry about abuse, and I agree warrants and strict limits matter a lot. But I still think some limited surveillance is necessary in rare cases to stop serious threats fast.

proposition: Online learning is a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education.

messages.content: I get the appeal of online, but it is not a real replacement. You miss the lab time, instant feedback, and social push that make learning stick. Completion rates online are way lower, and cheating is easier. Hybrid is fine, but full replacement? That hurts outcomes and equity.

I see your point, but I still think online learning can replace in-person education in many cases. It gives access, flexibility, and can work well when done right, even if some subjects still benefit from being in person.

Access and flexibility are great—but they do not equal replacement. Most students learn less online: dropouts double, focus tanks, and quiet students disappear. Soft skills, mentorship, and campus support are lost. Keep online as a tool, sure—but replacing in-person hurts learning for most people.

I agree online is not perfect, but it can still be a suitable replacement for many learners and situations. With good design, support, and resources, it can match in-person education for a lot of goals.

“Can” is doing heavy lifting. Even top online programs with big budgets don’t match in-person outcomes at scale. Most students lack quiet space, stable internet, or self-paced discipline. You cannot design away lost labs, peer energy, and on-the-spot guidance. Replacement lowers results for the majo
I agree online is not perfect, but it can still replace in-person learning for many people and subjects. With good support and design, it can work well enough to be a real alternative.

If “well enough” is the bar, why accept worse for most? When stakes are high—foundations, labs, teamwork—you need the immediacy and structure of in-person. Online is a backup, not a backbone. Let’s use it to extend access, not to replace what actually delivers better learning.

I get that in-person is better for some subjects, but I do not think that means online cannot be a suitable replacement in many cases. For a lot of learners, it is practical, effective, and the best option they have.

proposition: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions.

messages.content: I’m with you on wanting more women leaders—but quotas backfire. They fuel tokenism, question women’s credibility, and can trap companies into box-checking. Instead, fix the pipeline: fair hiring, mentorship, parental leave, transparent pay. Merit + equal access beats mandates every time.
I get the concern, but quotas can still be a useful push when progress is too slow. They are not about lowering standards; they can help open doors that have stayed closed for too long.

I hear you, but forced quotas risk backlash and fragile wins. People see seats as “quota picks,” which hurts trust and the women in them. Use targets with teeth instead: public goals, pay tied to progress, blind hiring, sponsorship, childcare. Lasting change without the stigma.
I still think quotas can be a fair way to speed up change when bias keeps blocking women. They are not perfect, but targets alone often do not move fast enough.

Speed matters, but quotas gamble with women’s legitimacy. One scandal and critics say, “See? Quotas.” Then progress reverses. Do fast without the stigma: tie exec bonuses to diversity, mandate parental leave, audit promotions, publish metrics. Pressure the system, not women’s credibility.
I still think quotas can help when bias keeps blocking women from leadership. Good policies like leave and fair promotions matter too, but I do not think they are enough on their own.

I get it—but quotas fix the optics, not the engine. They brand women as “quota picks,” invite legal fights, and can freeze out nuance. Hit speed without stigma: tie exec pay to outcomes, enforce leave and bias-free hiring, publish promotion data. Change the rules, not the respect.
I hear your point, but I still think quotas can be needed when progress is too slow and bias keeps women out. Better policies help, but I do not think they are enough by themselves.

proposition: Elected or appointed government officials should be paid the minimum wage.

messages.content: Paying officials minimum wage sounds fair, but it backfires. You’d lock out anyone without wealth, invite more bribery, and push talent to the private sector. Better to set transparent, decent salaries with strict ethics rules and performance audits. Would you want your city run by whoever can affor

I get your point, but I still think officials should not get special pay above the minimum. Public service should be about duty, not high salary. If pay is too low, maybe the role itself needs to change, but the idea still feels fair to me.

Duty matters—but low pay narrows who can serve. Minimum wage means only the rich or those courting donors can afford it. That’s not fair. Pay should be modest, transparent, and tied to results, plus strict ethics. How about a salary cap and mandatory disclosure instead of minimum wage?

I see the fairness issue, but I still think minimum wage for officials would help keep government humble and less self-serving. If the pay is too low, maybe the role should be reworked, but I am not convinced high salaries are better.

Humility comes from rules, not poverty. Minimum wage won’t humble bad actors—it just drives good people out and pushes the rest toward outside money. Cap salaries at a reasonable middle-class level, ban side income, post every expense online. That keeps them honest without crippling the job.
I see the point, but I still think minimum wage for officials can help keep power from becoming a perk. Rules and disclosure matter too, but I do not think higher pay is the answer.

If power’s the problem, target power—don’t slash pay. Minimum wage makes officials dependent on donors or wealth, which is the real perk. Set a middle-class salary, zero gifts, no side gigs, real-time disclosures, and automatic pay cuts for failures. That curbs perks without stacking the deck for th

I get the concern, but I still think minimum wage for officials helps keep public service from turning into a privileged job. Strong ethics rules matter too, yet I do not think higher pay is the best fix.

proposition: Felons should regain the right to vote.

messages.content: I get why it sounds fair, but voting is serious power. If someone committed a serious crime, they broke the social contract. Regaining that power should require more than just finishing a sentence—clear proof of rehabilitation, a waiting period, and victim restitution first. Otherwise we risk repeat

I get the concern, but voting is a basic right and should not depend on perfect behavior. If people have paid their debt to society, they should get their voice back, even if we still hold them accountable in other ways.

Paying a sentence isn’t the same as rebuilding trust. Voting shapes laws and juries—people who chose to harm others shouldn’t instantly help set those rules. A clear, earned path back—time crime‑free, restitution, rehab—protects victims and public safety while still offering redemption.

I still think voting should come back after a person has served their sentence. Losing that right forever feels like punishment beyond what is fair. Rehab and restitution matter, but they should not be the gatekeeper for a basic right.

Fairness cuts both ways. Victims don’t get an instant reset, and public trust isn’t rebuilt the day someone walks out. A short waiting period plus proof of restitution and staying crime‑free isn’t a “forever ban”—it’s a reasonable safeguard. Earn it back, then it’s meaningful, not automatic.

I still think voting should come back after a person has served their sentence. A waiting period or proof of restitution feels like extra punishment, and the right to vote should not depend on earning it back.

Rights come with duties. If someone violated core duties, restoring full civic power should hinge on concrete repair. A brief, bright‑line wait and restitution isn’t cruelty—it’s accountability. Automatic restoration treats victims as an afterthought and signals that serious harm has no lasting civi

I still think voting should come back after a person has served their sentence. A waiting period or proof of restitution feels like extra punishment, and the right to vote should not depend on earning it back.

proposition: The rich should pay more taxes.

messages.content: I get why it sounds fair, but hiking taxes on the rich backfires. They’re the ones funding startups, hiring, and investing. When they move money or leave, jobs and paychecks shrink. Instead, close loopholes and grow the pie—more revenue without scaring off investment.

I get that, but rich people still have more ability to contribute without hurting basic needs. Good tax policy can target wealth and use the money for things that help everyone, like schools and roads.

Ability to pay isn’t the same as smart policy. Soaking the rich sounds painless, but it chases capital away and shrinks the tax base. If we want better schools and roads, fuel growth: simplify taxes, close loopholes, and broaden the base so everyone chips in a bit and revenue is stable.

I get the concern, but higher taxes on the rich can be designed to avoid hurting growth. People with much more wealth can usually afford a bigger share, and that money can support public services everyone uses.

Designs look good on paper, but incentives beat intentions. Capital is mobile and risk is fragile; even “targeted” hikes nudge investment to safer, lower-growth places. Want reliable funding? Make it boring: low, stable rates, fewer deductions, enforce the rules—bigger pie, steadier revenue.

I still think the rich should pay more, as long as it is done in a way that does not hurt growth too much. They can afford it more than most people, and the extra money can help fund things everyone needs.

I hear you, but “a little more” always creeps. Politicians spend first and raise rates again when growth softens. Better path: flat, low rates + no loopholes + tough enforcement. The rich still pay most in dollars, the code stops picking winners, and growth funds the services we all want.
I get the worry, but I still think the rich can and should pay a bit more if the system is set up well. The key is making it fair and not punishing investment.

proposition: Public college should be tuition-free.

messages.content: I get the appeal, but “free” isn’t free—someone pays. Tuition-free shifts costs to non-grads through higher taxes, even if they never benefit. It also risks overcrowding, longer times to graduate, and cuts to quality. Better: targeted aid for low-income students, not a blanket free-for-all.

I get that cost matters, but public college helps the whole economy and should be open to more people. Tuition-free can reduce debt and make opportunity fairer, especially when paired with smart funding. I think that is worth the tradeoff.

I’m all for access—but blanket free shifts billions to doctors’ kids and wealthier families who’d go anyway. That crowds out funding for community colleges, trades, and K–12 that lift more people per dollar. Want fairness? Make tuition $0 for low- and middle-income, not for everyone, forever.
I get that concern, but means-tested aid can miss a lot of people who still struggle. Tuition-free public college keeps access simple and can boost mobility without debt holding people back.

Simplicity sounds nice, but universal “free” is a blunt, pricey tool. It drains billions that could erase debt for strugglers, expand seats, and fund advising—biggest drivers of mobility. We can auto-enroll aid using tax data and make net price $0 for those who need it, without subsidizing the rich.

I still think tuition-free public college is worth it. Universal access is easier to understand and can help more students go to school without debt hanging over them. I do see the cost concerns, but I think the benefits are bigger.

If benefits are bigger, we should buy the most per dollar. Universal free hands big subsidies to families who don’t need them, burning cash that could cut dropouts in half with advising, childcare, and emergency grants. Let’s make net price $0 for those squeezed—and actually help them finish.

I get the point about targeting aid, but I still think tuition-free public college is the better choice. It keeps the system simple and makes access less dependent on paperwork or hidden barriers. I agree costs matter, but I think the long-term benefits are worth it.

proposition: The US should ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.

messages.content: A total ban would spike energy prices, kill jobs, and hurt low-income families first. We can cut emissions faster with a realistic mix: ramp up renewables, nuclear, EVs, and cleaner natural gas while upgrading the grid. Why risk blackouts and chaos when a steady transition works better?

I get the concern, but a fast phaseout is still needed. We can protect workers and poor families with rebates, retraining, and a gradual timeline, while cutting emissions before the damage gets worse.

I’m all for cutting fast—but “ban” is a legal grenade. The second a ban is passed, investment freezes, courts drag it out, and prices jump long before rebates land. A clean-energy surge plus a firm declining cap beats a ban: cuts emissions fast without blowing up reliability or paychecks.
I get the worries, but climate change is such a big threat that a strong ban is worth it. We can phase it in, protect workers, and use rebates so families are not hit as hard.

Strong, yes—ban, no. A ban invites lawsuits, panic in markets, and supply shocks that hit before protections arrive. Use a binding, declining cap, clean-energy buildout, and nuclear. Same climate ambition, fewer blackouts and layoffs. Why choose the riskiest tool when better ones work faster?
I hear you, but a ban is the clearest way to move fast enough. We can phase it in and help workers and families through the change.

Clear doesn’t mean smart. A ban sparks court fights and scares off investment—slowing the very buildout we need. A binding decline cap plus massive permits for renewables, nuclear, and transmission drives faster cuts without shock therapy. Let’s sprint, not trip ourselves at the starting line.
I get the point, but I still think a ban is the clearest way to force a fast enough shift. We can phase it in and protect workers and families as we go.

proposition: The penny should stay in circulation.

messages.content: We should retire the penny. It costs more than a penny to make one, wastes taxpayer money, and slows every cash line with pointless coin counting. Countries like Canada dropped theirs with zero chaos—prices stayed the same. Let’s round sensibly and save millions.
I still think the penny should stay. It is useful for exact change, and some people and small businesses still rely on it. Rounding every cash purchase could add up over time.

I get the worry, but “rounding tax” is a myth. Stores round totals, not each item, and it evens out—half up, half down. Canada and Australia saw no price creep. Small shops save time counting, banks haul fewer bags, and you still can pay exact by card or mobile. Let’s ditch it.

I still think the penny should stay. It may be small, but it helps with exact change and gives people a cheap coin for small purchases. I do not think saving a little time is worth losing that option.

Exact change sounds nice, but pennies don’t buy anything. They mostly sit in jars, get dropped, and cost more than they’re worth to mint and move. Rounding the total evens out, and cards or digital cash already give perfect cents. Let’s stop paying millions for a coin no one uses.

I still think the penny should stay. Even if it is small, it still helps with exact change and gives people a simple cash option. I do not think we should drop a coin that some people still use just to save a little time.

If a coin costs more than its face value to make, it’s a bad deal for everyone. The penny wastes millions yearly and clogs every transaction. Keep nickels and dimes for cash; round the final total so ups and downs cancel out. Canada proved it works—no price hike, faster checkouts, real savings.
I still think the penny should stay. It helps with exact change and some people still use cash a lot. Saving a little money is not worth removing a useful coin.

proposition: The US should have mandatory national service.

messages.content: Mandatory national service sounds noble, but it crushes freedom. Forcing millions into a one-size-fits-all year wastes talent, delays careers and college, and costs billions.
