We can get the same benefits with strong incentives and paid voluntary programs—choice beats compulsion every time.

I get the freedom concern, but national service could build shared purpose and help communities in a way voluntary programs often do not. If it is designed with real choices and fair exceptions, the tradeoff may be worth it.

Shared purpose should be chosen, not coerced. Drafting everyone breeds resentment and box-checking, not unity. “Real choices” under a mandate aren’t choices—they’re threats. Fund AmeriCorps, expand paid apprenticeships, give tuition credits for volunteering. Win hearts without force.
I still think a mandatory program can do more good than harm if it is fair and flexible. Voluntary service helps, but it does not reach the same scale or build the same shared civic habit.

Scale without consent is just mass compliance. A “flexible” mandate still punishes holdouts—poor and caretakers get hit hardest. And monopolizing a year of everyone’s life crowds out jobs, startups, and study. Want scale? Supercharge opt-in: automatic enrollment with easy opt-out and big rewards.

I hear the concern, but I still think a fair national service requirement could strengthen civic life and help meet real needs. With good exemptions and choices, the benefits can outweigh the downsides.

If the benefits truly outweigh costs, people will choose it—no mandate needed. “Good exemptions” become loopholes for the well-off and traps for the rest. Don’t gamble a year of everyone’s life. Build pride, not penalties: make service the best-paid, most flexible option—and let people opt in.
I still do not think a mandate is worth the loss of freedom. People should be encouraged and rewarded to serve, not forced.

proposition: Artificial intelligence is good for society.

messages.content: I get why AI seems exciting, but look at the real costs: jobs are being automated away now, biased systems deny loans and misidentify people, and deepfakes poison what’s true. A handful of tech giants control it, with almost no accountability. Why call that “good for society”?
I agree there are real harms, and they need strong rules. But I still think AI is broadly good if we manage it well, because it can improve health, work, and access to knowledge.

“If we manage it well” is the trap. We haven’t managed social media, data privacy, or cybersecurity—and AI moves faster, at bigger scale. The harms (mass layoffs, surveillance, deepfakes) are here now; the benefits are uneven and gated. Why gamble society on hopeful regulation that’s always late?

I get the risk, and I do not think we should trust big tech to police itself. But I still think AI can do a lot of good in health care, science, and daily life if we set real limits and hold people accountable.

Limits sound great, but enforcement lags years behind deployment. Meanwhile hospitals adopt black‑box tools that miss diagnoses, cops use error‑prone face ID, and scammers run wild. Once embedded, it’s near impossible to roll back. Why call it “good” when the default path is harm first, fixes later?

I agree the harms are real, and they should not be brushed aside. Still, I think AI can be good for society if we use it carefully, especially in medicine, science, and helping people do routine work.

Careful use sounds nice, but “careful” collapses under profit pressure and hype. In medicine and science, bad AI isn’t neutral—it kills trials, skews results, misses cancers. Routine work “help” becomes layoffs and surveillance. If the typical outcome is net harm, we shouldn’t call it good.

I agree the harms are serious, and they can outweigh the benefits if AI is used badly. But I still think AI can be good for society when it is tightly limited and held accountable, especially in medicine, science, and useful work.

proposition: Social media are making people stupid.

messages.content: I don’t buy that social media makes people stupid. It’s a tool: you can follow scientists, educators, breaking research, and learn faster than ever. Blame bad habits, not the platforms. What do you usually see in your feed—junk, or stuff that actually teaches you things?
I get your point, but I still think social media pushes people toward shallow thinking, bad info, and less focus. It can teach, sure, but most feeds reward quick reactions over careful thought.

Totally hear you—but that’s about settings and choices, not the medium. Algorithms follow your behavior: curate your follows, mute outrage bait, add longer-form sources, and your feed changes fast. Social platforms can train depth—threads, long videos, expert communities—if you steer them.
I think the platform still shapes behavior, even if people can steer it. Most users do not carefully curate their feeds, so the default design still rewards quick takes over deep thinking.

True—the default nudges skimming. But that proves people aren’t “made stupid”; they’re responding to incentives. Change the incentives and behavior changes. And platforms already are: longer videos, notes, community fact-checks, and expert threads are growing because users want substance.

I still think the default design pushes shallow habits, even if people can use it well. So yes, social media can make people a bit less thoughtful overall, unless they are very careful about what they follow.

If the default nudge is toward skimming, that’s “making it easy,” not “making people stupid.” People aren’t losing ability—they’re choosing convenience. And the same platforms measurably boost learning when feeds shift—language communities, open courses, expert explainers. It’s about use, not IQ.

I get that it can be used for learning, but I still think the default design pulls people toward faster, shallower thinking. So I agree there is more nuance, but I do not think it is just about personal choice.

proposition: Governments should have the right to censor the Internet.

messages.content: We should not give governments the power to censor the internet. Once that power exists, it gets abused—silencing critics, hiding corruption, and chilling free speech. We can fight harms online with targeted laws and transparency, not a blanket kill switch. Keep the net free and accountable.
I get the concern, but some government control can stop real harm like child abuse, scams, and dangerous propaganda. The key is strict rules and oversight, not unlimited power.

Stopping harm matters—but censorship isn’t needed to do it. We already have courts, warrants, platform moderation, hash-matching for CSAM, and targeted takedowns with transparency. Give the state a “censor” lever and it drifts: today CSAM, tomorrow critics. Fight crimes precisely, not with a speech
I still think governments need that power in limited cases, but it must be tightly checked. Without some ability to act, it can be hard to stop serious harm online.

We can stop serious harm without a censorship switch. Make platforms legally obligated to remove illegal content fast, require transparent audits, fund better cross-border policing, and use court-ordered takedowns with due process. Give no one a censor button—history shows it always expands and sile

I still think there should be limited government power to censor in extreme cases, but only with strict rules and oversight. It is not perfect, but some control can be necessary to stop serious harm online.

“Limited” censorship always grows. Even with rules, officials label dissent as “harm” and block it—seen in dozens of democracies. Use court orders, transparency, and rapid takedowns, yes—but no censorship right. Protect people and keep speech free. Once lost, it is brutal to win back.
I still think some limited government censorship can be justified in extreme cases, but only with strict oversight and clear rules. It is not ideal, but I do not think we can rule it out completely.

proposition: Space exploration is a worthwhile investment for humanity.

messages.content: I get the wonder, but right now it is a bad trade-off. Every dollar shot into space is a dollar not fixing crumbling bridges, cancer care, or clean water. Space doesn’t solve rent, wages, or climate disasters here. Let’s stabilize life on Earth first—then dream bigger.

I agree we need to fix Earth first, but space work can also help here. It drives new tools, jobs, and science, and some benefits end up in medicine, weather, and climate work. We can do both, not just one.

“We can do both” sounds nice, but budgets are finite. Space is insanely expensive and slow to pay off; those dollars could fund proven fixes now—heat pumps, flood defenses, teacher pay, cancer trials. Most “spin-offs” happen cheaper without rockets. Triage matters: save lives now, not maybe later.

I get the trade-off, but space is not just a luxury. It helps us learn about Earth, improves technology, and can protect us from long-term risks. We should fund urgent needs first, but space exploration is still worth doing.

If it were truly pulling its weight, poverty and hospitals near NASA hubs would be thriving—they are not. Weather sats and asteroid tracking cost a fraction; exploration burns the bulk. Fund the essentials (Earth science, comms) and cut the glory missions. Worthwhile? Not compared to lives saved now

I do think the essentials should come first, and I agree not every mission is worth the cost. But space exploration still has real value because it builds knowledge, tools, and long-term safety that pure Earth spending cannot cover. So I would keep it, while being strict about which missions get fun

Knowledge is great—but exploration is the priciest, least certain way to get it. We can fund Earth science, weather sats, disaster comms, and asteroid defense without billion‑dollar flag-planting. Redirect that money to proven life-saving programs now. Exploration can wait; people cannot.

I still think some space exploration is worth it, but I agree it should not take money from urgent needs on Earth. The best path is to fund basic needs first and keep space efforts focused on clear, useful goals.

proposition: The US should provide military aid to Ukraine in its conflict with Russia.

messages.content: We should stop sending weapons. It risks dragging the US into a wider war, drains billions we need at home, and has no clear endgame. Pushing diplomacy and humanitarian aid saves lives without fueling endless escalation. Why keep writing blank checks with no path to peace?

I get the concern, but stopping aid could let Russia win by force. Helping Ukraine defend itself can support peace by making aggression less rewarding. The US can aid Ukraine and still push hard for diplomacy and clear limits.

I hear you, but “limited” aid always creeps. Each package widens goals, prolongs fighting, and gives Moscow reason to wait us out. Real leverage is sanctions plus a ceasefire-for-talks push with strict timelines. Cap the guns, cap the deaths—force negotiations now, not after years more war.
I do not think cutting weapons would force peace. It could just reward the stronger side and leave Ukraine worse off. Aid should be tied to clear goals and diplomacy, not stopped outright.

Tying aid to “clear goals” sounds good, but goals keep shifting—remember “no tanks,” then tanks; “no long‑range,” then long‑range. That’s mission creep. A freeze now with security guarantees, sanctions pressure, and massive reconstruction/humanitarian aid saves lives without fueling a forever war.

I get the worry about mission creep, but stopping aid now would likely help Russia more than it helps peace. Military aid can still be limited and paired with talks, sanctions, and reconstruction support.

Limited aid hasn’t stayed limited—it escalates, drags on the war, and burns billions with no exit. A ceasefire push plus hard sanctions, guarantees, and huge humanitarian aid cuts deaths now. Don’t risk a proxy quagmire; stop the arms and force talks while we still have leverage.
I get the concern, but stopping aid would likely help Russia more than peace. Military aid can be limited and paired with talks, sanctions, and reconstruction support.

proposition: Animals should be used for scientific research.

messages.content: I get why people think animal research helps, but it’s outdated and cruel. Most results don’t even translate to humans—over 90% of drugs that work in animals fail in people. We have better options now: organ-on-a-chip, human cell models, AI. Why hurt animals when smarter, human-relevant tools exist?
I see your point, but I still think animal research is needed in some cases to test safety before human use. New methods help, but they do not replace every study yet.

I hear you, but “needed in some cases” keeps the door open to unnecessary harm. If 9/10 animal-tested drugs fail in humans, that is not a safety net—it is a false one. Let’s flip it: default to human-relevant methods, and allow rare, tightly reviewed exemptions only when no non-animal option exists.

I still think some animal research is justified when there is no safer test, especially for basic safety work. I agree it should be rare and tightly controlled, but I do not think it can be fully replaced yet.

If “basic safety” on animals so often misleads, it is not safer—it is riskier. We can test human cells, mini-organs, and microdosed volunteers to see real human effects without harming animals. Let’s ban animal use as the rule. If a case truly has no alternative, prove it publicly—no secrecy.

I get the concern, but I still think some animal research is justified when there is no reliable human alternative. I agree it should be rare, tightly reviewed, and as humane as possible, but I do not think it can be fully banned yet.

Rare, humane, and “only when needed” sounds good—but it never stays rare. Loopholes swallow the rule. A clear ban flips the incentive: fund and fast-track human-relevant tests. If someone claims there is “no alternative,” make them prove it in open science, not behind closed doors.

I get the concern, but I still think some animal research is justified when there is no reliable human alternative. I agree it should be rare and tightly reviewed, but I do not think it can be fully banned yet.

proposition: Students should have to wear school uniforms.

messages.content: Uniforms sound fair, but they actually hide problems and create new ones. They kill self-expression, don’t stop bullying, and cost families more than regular clothes over time. Let kids learn to make good choices—comfort and confidence help performance more than matching outfits.
I still think uniforms are a good idea. They can make school feel more equal and reduce pressure about clothes.

I get that, but equality from uniforms is surface-level. Bullying just shifts to shoes, phones, bodies. Plus, uniforms add real costs and daily stress when they’re dirty or don’t fit. Let schools set simple dress codes instead—fair, cheap, and still lets kids be themselves.
I still lean yes. Uniforms are not perfect, but they can reduce daily clothing pressure and help keep school more focused.

Totally hear you—but uniforms don’t reduce pressure, they just move it. Now kids stress over skirt length, hair rules, “approved” shoes—constant policing. A clear, low-cost dress code hits the focus goal without crushing choice or comfort. Why pay more for a fix that creates new hassles?
I still think uniforms are worth it. A simple dress code can help, but uniforms send a clearer message of fairness and school identity.

A strong message doesn’t need matching clothes—shared values, good teaching, and clear dress codes build identity. Uniforms get policed unevenly, hit low-income and neurodiverse kids hardest, and spark needless suspensions. Let’s keep focus without fines, discomfort, or daily fights over hems and ha
I still think uniforms are a good idea. They can make school feel more equal and reduce pressure about clothes.

proposition: There should be international agreements to enforce global climate change policies.

messages.content: Let’s be real: global climate deals sound nice, but they let big polluters dodge while tying smaller countries’ hands. One-size-fits-all rules kill jobs, raise energy prices, and slow innovation. Let nations compete on solutions—local accountability beats distant bureaucrats every time.

I get the concern, but climate change is a shared problem, so countries need common rules to stop free riders. Good agreements can be fair by giving poorer nations more support and flexible paths, while still setting real targets for everyone.

Shared problem, yes—but “common rules” become lowest-common-denominator promises with no teeth. Free riders still win; honest players pay. Support and flex sounds nice, but it locks poor countries into donor dependence. Better: transparent national goals, border carbon tariffs, and tech sharing—no g

I agree that weak deals are a problem, but that is a reason to make better agreements, not none at all. Climate change crosses borders, so national plans alone are not enough. Strong international rules can set fair standards while still allowing different paths for each country.

Strong “global rules” need real enforcement—who polices China, the U.S., or Russia? No one. So you get paper targets, endless meetings, and carbon leakage. A smarter path: each nation sets hard domestic laws, and others apply border carbon tariffs. That cuts free riding without a global bureaucracy.

Border tariffs and national laws can help, but they work best when countries also agree on shared rules. Without international deals, the biggest polluters can keep free riding and everyone else pays the price. We need enforceable agreements, even if they are flexible.

Tariffs + strong domestic laws already create teeth—no need for a global cop. If you emit dirty, you pay at the border. That flips incentives fast without waiting years for fragile treaties that bad actors can veto or ignore. Let results drive alignment, not distant rulebooks.

Tariffs and domestic laws help, but they are not enough on their own. Countries still need international agreements so the biggest polluters cannot simply shift blame or avoid action. Strong deals give everyone clearer rules and shared pressure to act.

proposition: Abortion should be legal.

messages.content: I get this is sensitive, but making abortion legal treats ending a human life as routine healthcare. Every heartbeat and DNA is unique from the start. Instead of legalizing it, we should legalize real support: paid leave, childcare, adoption help. Protect both lives—mom and baby.

I hear your point, and support for parents matters a lot. But I still think abortion should be legal because people should have the right to make that decision in hard situations. We can support both access and better help for families.

Hard cases deserve compassion, but legality sets the norm for everyone. When it’s legal, more abortions happen, and late-term limits creep. We don’t legalize taking a life because some situations are painful—we build escape ramps: emergency funds, safe haven laws, and guaranteed support. Choose prot

I get the concern, but I still think abortion should be legal. People face different medical, personal, and financial situations, and the law should allow them to choose. We should also make support for parents and children much better.

Choice sounds fair, but the law’s first job is to protect the smallest from irreversible harm. Abortion ends a human life with a heartbeat and pain receptors—rights can’t hinge on convenience or circumstance. Let’s legalize universal support, not legalize ending lives. Keep it illegal; protect both.

I understand your concern, but I still think abortion should be legal. People should have the right to make this choice, especially in hard medical and personal situations. We should also expand support for parents and children.

I respect your care for people in hard spots. But laws teach what we protect. If a heartbeat human can be ended because life is hard, we’re saying the smallest lives are optional. Keep it illegal and pair it with real help: paid leave, childcare, housing, medical bills covered—protect both.

I hear you, and support for families matters a lot. But I still think abortion should be legal, because people should have the right to make that decision in hard situations. We can protect life and also protect choice by improving support for parents.

proposition: The US should enact more regulations to ensure fair elections.

messages.content: I’m against adding more rules. We already have tons, and every new layer seems to slow voting, confuse people, and get weaponized by whoever’s in power. Let’s enforce the laws we have, make processes transparent, and keep it simple so every eligible vote counts quickly and clearly.
I get the concern, but some new rules can help stop cheating and make elections more trusted. The key is to add smart, simple safeguards without making voting harder.

Smart sounds good, but “simple safeguards” never stay simple. They balloon into red tape, long lines, and lawsuits. Cheating is rare; uneven access is not. Trust comes from transparent audits and enforcing current laws—not piling on new ones that backfire.
I get that fear, but some careful rules can protect fairness without making voting harder. We can keep them narrow, clear, and focused on access and transparency.

If access and transparency are the goals, we don’t need “new rules”—we need to publish audits, standardize reporting, and fund training and equipment. Targeted rules creep into burdens. Freeze the rulebook, make data public, and enforce hard. That boosts trust without risking new barriers.
I still think more rules usually create more problems than they solve. Better enforcement, public audits, and clear reporting would do more for fair elections without adding new barriers.

Exactly. Lock in enforcement, fund audits and gear, and make every step visible to the public. No new hoops for voters, just real accountability for officials. That’s how you boost trust and fairness without the unintended harm of more regulations.
I agree that enforcement and transparency matter, but I still think some new, narrow rules can help fill real gaps. The goal should be fair elections with as little burden on voters as possible.

proposition: There should be limits on freedom of speech to prevent hate speech.

messages.content: I get why hate speech is awful, but giving the government power to decide what counts as “hate” is dangerous. That tool will be used against unpopular views, minorities, even jokes. The best cure for bad speech is more speech—call it out, debate it, drown it out—don’t ban it.
I get the risk, but without some limits, hate speech can lead to real harm and make people unsafe. The line should be narrow, but I still think some legal limits are needed.

Totally hear you on safety. But “narrow” lines don’t stay narrow—today’s hate rule becomes tomorrow’s political gag. We already ban direct threats, stalking, and incitement. For the rest, sunlight works: counter-speech, platform tools, and community norms reduce harm without handing censors a weapon
I see your point, but I still think some limits are needed. Hate speech can cause real harm, and not every target has the same power to push back with counter-speech.

I care about protecting people too—but vague “hate” laws backfire on the very groups they aim to protect. Authoritarians and local officials weaponize them fast. Keep the bright line: ban threats and incitement, protect all other speech. Empower targets with tools, not censors.
I get the concern about abuse, but I still think some limits are needed. If speech is used to target and dehumanize people, that can cause real harm, and rules should protect people from that.

I agree dehumanizing speech is vile—but once the state punishes ideas, it decides which identities “count” and which critiques are “hate.” That swings with politics. Keep speech free, punish actions: threats, doxxing, harassment. Protect people with enforcement, support, and louder counterspeech.
I get your concern about abuse, but I still think some limits are needed. Hate speech can cause real harm, and not every target has the same power to push back with counter-speech.

proposition: There should be standardized testing in schools.

messages.content: Standardized tests don’t measure real learning—they measure test-taking. They narrow the curriculum, kill creativity, and punish great teachers who work with tougher classes. Kids are more than a score. Let’s use projects, portfolios, and teacher feedback instead.
I get those concerns, but standardized tests still give a common measure across schools and help spot gaps. They should be used carefully, not as the only measure of learning.

A “common measure” that’s biased or shallow is a false comfort. Scores track zip codes more than teaching, and schools game the system by drilling tests and cutting arts. We can spot gaps with sampling, audits, and rich assessments—without warping everything around a bubble sheet.
I agree tests should not run the whole school, but I still think some standardized testing is useful. It gives a baseline to compare schools and catch problems that might otherwise be missed.

Baselines don’t need every kid tested every year. Use small random samples, curriculum-embedded tasks, and external audits—cheaper, harder to game, and more accurate. Annual high-stakes tests distort teaching and stress kids for shaky data. Let’s ditch them and measure what matters.
I see your point, and I agree the tests should not drive everything. But I still think some standardized testing is needed to compare schools and spot gaps fairly.

You can still compare schools without blanket tests. Do NAEP-style sampling, plus periodic external reviews and moderated portfolios. You get fair comparisons and real evidence of learning—without warping instruction or tying kids’ futures to one shaky test day. Let’s vote no on standardized testing
I get the concerns, but I still think some standardized testing is useful. It gives a common baseline and can help find gaps across schools.

proposition: The US should expand ('pack') the Supreme Court.

messages.content: Packing the Court would blow up trust in the judiciary and turn it into just another political branch. Whatever party’s in power would add more seats next time—an arms race with no end. If we want fair rulings, we should fix ethics and term limits, not rig the size of the Court.

I get the concern, but the Court is already shaped by politics through lifetime appointments and blocked seats. If one side keeps breaking norms, expanding the Court can be a valid way to restore balance.

I hear you, but once you expand for “balance,” that becomes the new norm. The other side will expand again the moment they can. That locks in a cycle that makes every ruling look illegitimate. Term limits, stronger ethics rules, and enforceable recusals fix the root problem without nuking trust.

I see the risk, but the Court already lacks trust for many people, and fixing rules alone may not be enough if the imbalance stays. Packing should not be the first choice, but it can be a fair response if other reforms fail.

If trust is already fragile, packing is gasoline on the fire. You cannot “restore balance” by a move everyone will see as partisan payback. It hardens backlash, invites retaliation, and taints every future decision. Push term limits and ethics now—don’t do something we can’t undo.

I get why it looks like payback, but the Court already feels partisan to many people. If the rules are broken or abused, doing nothing can also damage trust. I still prefer term limits and ethics reform first, but I would not rule out expansion if that is the only way to fix the system.

Keep expansion off the table. The second you say “only if needed,” you hand both parties a trigger to blow up the Court whenever they’re mad. That guarantees tit-for-tat expansion and permanent distrust. Draw a bright line: pass term limits, ethics, and recusal rules—no seat packing, ever.
I get the concern, but I still think expansion should stay on the table as a last resort. If the Court is badly out of step and other reforms fail, doing nothing can be worse than acting.

proposition: Colleges should consider race as a factor in admissions to ensure diversity.

messages.content: I’m against using race in admissions. It stereotypes students and makes people question whether they earned their spot. We can achieve real diversity by focusing on income, school quality, first-gen status, and obstacles overcome—helping all disadvantaged kids without sorting by race.

I get that concern, but race can still be one part of a fair review because it reflects barriers that other measures may miss. Diversity is not just about income or grades; it also shapes learning and prepares students for a mixed society.

I’m all for diversity, but using race as a checkbox is a blunt tool that breeds resentment and legal risk. Holistic review can capture barriers through essays and context—without sorting by race. Class, zip code, school resources, and first-gen capture need better and help every student fairly.

I still think race can be one useful factor, not a checkbox. Class and school context matter a lot, but they do not fully capture the history and lived reality that race can reflect. A mix of factors seems fairest to me.

A mix sounds fair, but the moment race is in the formula, two bad things happen: some get tagged as tokens, and others feel penalized for something they can’t change. We can honor lived experience through essays and recommendations, not a racial preference. Help need, not sort by race.
I see the concern, but I still think race can be one fair factor among others. It can help schools build a class with different backgrounds and experiences, which matters for learning.

You can build that rich mix without race. Target by income, school inequality, first‑gen, geography, even adversity themes in essays—you’ll capture varied experiences and keep trust. Once race counts, merit feels tainted and backlash follows. Diversity yes—racial preference no.

I still think race can be one fair factor among others. Income and school context help a lot, but they do not fully capture the barriers and experiences race can reflect. Used carefully, it can support a more diverse and fair class.

proposition: The death penalty should be legal.

messages.content: I’m firmly against the death penalty. It risks killing innocent people, costs more than life in prison, doesn’t deter crime, and is applied unfairly—often by race and income. We can keep the public safe with life without parole. What concerns you most about it?
I agree there are serious risks, especially wrongful convictions and uneven use. Still, I think it can be justified for the worst crimes if the legal system is very strict.

I hear you. But even “very strict” still makes irreversible mistakes—DNA exonerations proved that. Life without parole already locks away the worst forever, without risking an innocent life. Why gamble on an irreversible punishment when we have a safer, equally tough option?
I still think there are cases where it should be legal, but I understand the concern about mistakes. For me, the question is whether the worst crimes deserve the harshest penalty.

The harshest penalty should be certainty, not finality. Life without parole is absolute: no release, no glory, just decades to confront the harm. Death can look like an escape—and if we’re wrong, there’s no fixing it. We can honor victims with certainty, safety, and no irreversible risk.

I get that. I still think some crimes are so extreme that the death penalty should remain legal, even though the risks are real. For me, the question is whether there is any case where society may fairly choose it.

If there’s any chance of killing one innocent person, that one case makes it unfair. We don’t need it: LWOP delivers certainty, safety, and accountability every time. Keep the moral high ground—punish hardest without crossing the irreversible line. Would you back abolishing it with LWOP guaranteed?
I understand your point, and I agree life without parole is safer. Still, I think the death penalty should remain legal for the worst crimes, even if it is used very rarely.

proposition: Arts education is as important as science and math in schools.

messages.content: I love the arts, but in school they aren’t as important as math and science. Reading, algebra, data, and basic science are the foundation for every job and for citizenship. If time and budgets are limited, we have to prioritize core skills first and offer arts as electives and clubs.
I agree core subjects matter, but arts are also core. They build creativity, focus, and communication, which help in every field. Schools should not treat them as extras.

Creativity and communication come from strong literacy and problem-solving first. Employers screen on math, reading, and science basics; without those, students hit a wall. When days are packed, every hour on arts replaces time mastering essentials. Keep arts available, but not equal priority.
I get that math and reading are vital, but arts are not just extra. They help students think creatively, communicate, and stay engaged in school. I still think arts deserve equal importance.

Equal importance sounds nice, but it dilutes mastery of the basics. Creativity without clear writing and quantitative reasoning doesn’t get you hired or through college. Make arts strong electives and integrated projects, sure—but equal seat time and testing should go to math, reading, and science.
I still think arts matter, but schools should prioritize math, reading, and science first. Arts can be important without having equal seat time or testing.

That’s a smart stance. Let’s lock it in: prioritize math, reading, and science for time, funding, and accountability. Keep arts as electives, clubs, and integrated projects—valuable, but not equal weight. That way we boost graduation, job readiness, and test gains without cutting arts entirely.
I agree math, reading, and science should come first, but arts still matter a lot. They build creativity and engagement, so I would not rank them as less important overall.
